course_title: Integrating Quantum Processing Units into Data Center Infrastructure
course_subtitle: An OCP Academy course on deploying QPUs as hybrid data center infrastructure.

term_glossary.tooltip: A quantum processing unit: the subsystem where quantum information is prepared, manipulated, and measured.
The physical implementation used to realize quantum information, such as superconducting circuits, photonics, trapped ions, neutral atoms, silicon spin, or diamond NV centers.
Quantum error correction: methods that detect and correct errors in quantum states using classical feedback and redundant physical qubits.
A fault-tolerant computational unit encoded across many physical qubits using error-correction mechanisms.
The responsibility boundary between facility/operator and tenant/system integrator for deployment, operations, and maintenance.
A closed-cycle cryogenic cooling component, often using helium, that supports low-temperature operation.
A cryogenic system using helium-3 and helium-4 behavior to reach millikelvin temperatures.
Quantum as a Service: an access model where users consume quantum computing resources through a provider platform or facility.
term_glossary.reference_label: 2026 OCP EMEA Summit FTS Quantum Track

slides.title: Why OCP Is Organizing This Work
slides.subtitle: The workstream creates common language across facilities, vendors, and modalities.

bullets.text: The whitepaper gathers input from QPU vendors, data center stakeholders, and OCP contributors.
The work covers facility envelopes and infrastructure interfaces.
The goal is to reduce bespoke negotiation as production deployments scale.

figure.caption: The workstream links community discussion, the whitepaper, summit material, and future deployment checklists.

slides.title: The Market Is Moving Before Standards Are Finished
slides.subtitle: Market attention makes deployment discipline more urgent, not less.

bullets.text: Hardware, facility, cloud, and service providers all shape how users reach quantum resources.
The summit market outlook framed 2026 as a year of increased quantum visibility.
Operators need clearer envelopes, not vague promises.

figure.alt: Investment trend chart from the OCP QPU integration whitepaper
figure.caption: Figure 1 from the paper: capital deployment is presented as following an AI-like trajectory with a maturity offset.

source_refs.source: Quantum is Not an Island Market Outlook for 2026+.pptx
Quantum is Not an Island Market Outlook for 2026+.pptx

slides.title: Quantum Is Not a Standalone Island
slides.subtitle: Near-term value depends on QPUs working beside classical infrastructure.

bullets.text: Quantum does not replace all classical computing or accelerate every problem.
Workflows still need orchestration, preprocessing, post-processing, storage, and control.
Placement, latency, power, cooling, and network design affect usefulness.

figure.caption: A QPU is one specialized resource inside a larger CPU, GPU, HPC, AI, storage, network, and control environment.

slides.title: Modalities Drive Infrastructure Choices
slides.subtitle: The physical implementation changes the facility conversation.

cards.title: Cryogenic systems
cards.body: Superconducting, annealing, and silicon spin systems can require dilution refrigerators and millikelvin operation.

cards.title: Laser and vacuum systems
cards.body: Neutral atom and trapped-ion systems depend on optical stability, vacuum infrastructure, and environmental control.

cards.title: Photonic appliances
cards.body: Fiber-based photonic systems can align closely with rack-level data center deployment.

cards.title: Diamond and NV centers
cards.body: Room-temperature and cryogenic diamond approaches shift attention to EMI, optics, and magnetic stability.

source_refs.source: Quantum is Not an Island Market Outlook for 2026+.pptx

slides.title: Module 1 Summary
items: Quantum deployment is becoming an infrastructure problem.
OCP is creating common language across facilities, vendors, and modalities.
QPU systems are expected to work with classical compute, not replace it.
Modality differences drive power, cooling, control, space, and environmental needs.

slides.title: Check Your Hybrid Integration Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Why is QPU integration a data center infrastructure topic?

choices.text: QPUs rely on classical compute, storage, networking, controls, and facilities.
QPUs replace all CPU and GPU workloads.
choices.feedback_incorrect: This overstates QPU use. The course frames QPUs as specialized resources beside classical infrastructure.

choices.text: Every QPU modality has identical site needs.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Different modalities can drive different power, cooling, access, and environmental requirements.

choices.text: Standards work is already finished.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The workstream exists because shared language and repeatable deployment practice are still maturing.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The key idea is hybrid integration: QPUs become operational resources only when the surrounding classical and facility stack is planned with them.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the hybrid integration idea. The strongest answer connects the QPU to classical systems and facility services.

questions.prompt: Which planning signals support the OCP workstream focus? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Hybrid QPU deployments need common service and facility language.
Modality choices change power, cooling, access, and environment.
Data center planning horizons can outlast quantum product cycles.
Application teams can plan quantum deployments alone.
Every QPU can be treated as a generic rack server.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The workstream is about shared infrastructure planning before deployments become one-off exceptions.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for signals that affect infrastructure planning, not only application demand or generic server assumptions.

slides.next_module_title: The Logical QPU and Its Classical Stack
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 1: Why Quantum Belongs in the Data Center.

modules.title: The Logical QPU and Its Classical Stack
modules.subtitle: Reference architecture, classical dependencies, storage growth, and deployment coupling.

slides.title: Inside the Hybrid QPU Stack
slides.subtitle: How physical quantum hardware connects to control, compute, storage, and applications.

slides.title: Architecture Elements You Will Classify
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to...
objectives: Identify the main components of the logical QPU reference stack.
Explain why classical infrastructure is part of quantum performance.
Use syndrome-data examples to reason about storage demand.
Compare standalone, co-located, and on-node deployment models.

slides.title: From Physical QPU to Application Interface

figure.alt: Full-stack architecture of a quantum computer
figure.caption: Figure 2 from the paper: the full-stack view connects physical QPU hardware, control systems, middleware, and application interfaces.

slides.caption: A data-center deployment must evaluate the complete stack, not only the physical quantum processor.

slides.title: The Four Components of a Logical QPU

figure.caption: Figure 3 from the paper: the reference stack highlights the real-time host, interconnect, quantum system controller, and physical QPU.

slides.caption: The reference stack is a planning model. Not every near-term deployment needs every component.

slides.title: Classical Compute Is in the Critical Path
slides.subtitle: AI acceleration, control, and QEC have different latency and placement needs.

bullets.text: Supports resource allocation, transpilation, queue optimization, post-selection, and mitigation.
Enables discovery, allocation, scheduling, observability, and hybrid participation.
Requires low-latency accelerator resources close to the pulse-processing path.

figure.alt: Quantum job workflow in an HPC environment
figure.caption: Figure 5 from the paper: the workflow emphasizes that classical systems handle most actions around the quantum execution step.

slides.title: Syndrome Data Changes Storage Planning
slides.subtitle: Continuous correction telemetry can dominate storage needs.

bullets.text: Future fault-tolerant systems could generate approximately 100 terabytes per second of error-correction data.
One logical qubit example produces about 345 GB per day of raw metadata.
Retention may include syndrome history, calibration traces, diagnostics, provenance, and audit records.

figure.caption: Storage scales with qubit-hours and operational telemetry, not only user job output.
figure.svg:

  <defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L7,3 z" fill="#8DC63F"/></marker></defs>

  <rect x="40" y="76" width="194" height="102" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="3"/><text x="137" y="116" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#333333">1 logical qubit</text><text x="137" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062"><tspan x="137" dy="0">encoded over 1,000</tspan><tspan x="137" dy="17">physical qubits</tspan></text><path d="M 236 127 L 250 127" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="256" y="76" width="194" height="102" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="352" y="116" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#333333">4 MB/s</text><text x="352" y="146" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">raw syndrome metadata</text><path d="M 452 127 L 465 127" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="471" y="76" width="194" height="102" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="568" y="116" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#333333">345 GB/day</text><text x="568" y="146" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">per logical qubit</text><path d="M 668 127 L 680 127" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="686" y="76" width="194" height="102" rx="10" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="3"/><text x="783" y="116" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="20" font-weight="700" fill="#333333">100 TB/s</text><text x="783" y="146" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">future FTQC estimate</text>
</svg>

slides.title: Three Deployment Coupling Models
slides.subtitle: Coupling determines latency, complexity, and facility consequences.

cards.title: Standalone
cards.body: QPU is housed independently and accessed remotely. Lower integration burden, higher hybrid-workflow latency.

cards.title: Co-located
cards.body: QPU is positioned near classical compute. Better local connectivity with manageable integration complexity.

cards.title: On-node
cards.body: QPU is embedded in a classical node. Tightest coupling and lowest latency, with the highest design complexity.

slides.title: Module 2 Summary
items: A QPU system depends on a full classical and control stack.
The logical QPU reference architecture provides a planning vocabulary.
QEC and control loops can create tight latency and storage demands.
Deployment coupling changes rack layout, networking, cooling, service access, and orchestration.

slides.title: Check Your Logical QPU Stack
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which classical elements belong in the logical QPU deployment path?

choices.text: Real-time hosts, interconnects, controllers, QEC compute, storage, and application interfaces.
Only the qubit device and its immediate enclosure.
choices.feedback_incorrect: That is too narrow. The logical QPU stack includes classical control, compute, storage, and interfaces.

choices.text: Only the user portal.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The user interface matters, but placement, latency, control, and data movement still shape the deployment.

choices.text: Only cooling and power.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Facility services are important, but the logical QPU also depends on classical compute, control, interconnect, and storage.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Planning has to include the classical stack around the physical QPU, not just the quantum device.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for the answer that treats the physical QPU and its classical support systems as one deployable stack.

questions.prompt: Which choices can affect QPU placement and performance? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Latency between the QPU, controls, and classical compute.
Where QEC and storage pipelines run.
Whether deployment is standalone, co-located, or on-node.
Treating the QPU as an isolated appliance.
Only the name of the user-facing service portal.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Placement decisions connect latency, control, storage, and the coupling model.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for choices that change timing, data movement, or deployment coupling.

slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 2: The Logical QPU and Its Classical Stack.

modules.title: Facility Readiness by Modality
modules.subtitle: Site selection, service boundaries, environmental controls, and modality-specific requirements.
modules.next_module_title: From Pilot Deployment to OCP Practice

slides.title: The Facility Envelope for Quantum
slides.subtitle: What the site must provide so QPUs can arrive, operate, and be serviced.

slides.title: Readiness Factors You Will Compare
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to...
objectives: Classify site-selection factors for QPU deployment.
Interpret service boundaries and demarcations for quantum systems.
Compare modality envelopes using power, cooling, sensitivity, and maintenance categories.
Identify facility-level risks that require vendor and operator coordination.

slides.title: Site Selection Starts Outside the Rack
slides.subtitle: External conditions set the baseline for the operating envelope.

bullets.text: Seismic activity, climate, and nearby transport can affect stability.
Vibration, magnetic fields, acoustic profiles, and EMI must be characterized.
Delivery paths, staging, floor loading, power, cooling, and access all need early review.

figure.alt: Site readiness rings for QPU deployment
figure.caption: Site selection includes geography, vibration and EMI, structure, delivery, utilities, and environmental stability.

slides.title: Service Boundaries Need New Precision
slides.subtitle: Quantum deployments can cross the usual rack-level boundary.

bullets.text: Power, cooling, space, water, access, and building systems require explicit ownership.
QPU equipment, control electronics, vendor service, calibration, and consumables may require special access.
Alarms, incidents, maintenance, and change windows must be documented before delivery.

figure.caption: Clear handoffs reduce uncertainty around power, water, cryogens, vendor access, shielding, alarms, and maintenance.
figure.svg:

slides.title: Read the Modality Matrix Like an Operator
slides.subtitle: The table translates physics choices into facility planning categories.
rows: Optics, lasers, thermal stability, and vibration matter.
Lower power does not remove EMI and optical-stability questions.
Appliance-like deployment can still include detector cryogenics.
Cryogenics, water, floor loading, maintenance, and restart behavior matter.

slides.title: Laser and Vacuum Systems Change Room Protocols
slides.subtitle: Neutral atom and trapped-ion deployments make room conditions part of system reliability.
rows: 1.5 m hallway, 1.2 m to 2 m doorway, 40 m2 setup area
40 m2 to 70 m2 setup area around optical table
20 C to 24 C, 30% to 50% RH, 12.5 um/s vibration target
20 C to 21 C, 30% to 50% RH by vendor, 50 um/s vibration example
Vendor or certified maintenance; frequent calibration activities
Pre-deployment survey, 6 to 8 week deployment window, optical and environmental stability

slides.title: Photonic QPUs Can Look Like Appliances
slides.subtitle: Fiber-integrated photonic systems can align closely with rack-level operations.

bullets.text: Time-bin encoding and fiber-integrated components support robust deployment outside labs.
The ORCA example uses two standard 19-inch racks, approximately 800 kg combined.
The whitepaper describes about 2 days for rack placement, power, and network integration.

figure.alt: ORCA photonic quantum system deployed in a data center
figure.caption: Figure 9 from the paper: photonic QPUs can be delivered as integrated rack-level appliances while still depending on precise optical and detector subsystems.

slides.title: Cryogenic Modalities Bring Plant-Level Questions
slides.subtitle: Millikelvin operation connects QPU performance to power, water, floor loading, and service processes.

bullets.text: Superconducting gate-based QPUs operate at 10 to 20 mK.
Examples include 30 kW peak electrical details, 1000 kg/m2 floor support, water constraints, and 25% to 60% RH.
Interruptions can force recalibration or recovery windows that may exceed 48 hours for some platforms.

figure.alt: Dilution refrigerator examples from the whitepaper
figure.caption: Figure 10 from the paper: dilution refrigerators and supporting plant equipment make cryogenic QPU deployments a facility-level planning topic.

slides.title: Module 3 Summary
items: Site selection must account for geography, vibration, EMI, climate, delivery, utilities, and structure.
Quantum deployments need explicit service boundaries for facility and integrator responsibilities.
Modality differences change power, cooling, environmental, maintenance, and consumable requirements.
Facilities must protect the QPU from disturbance and protect surrounding infrastructure from QPU effects.

slides.title: Check Your Facility Readiness Lens
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the first planning move when comparing QPU modalities?

choices.text: Translate the modality into site, environment, service, and recovery requirements.
Assume every modality fits existing rack and room practices.
choices.feedback_incorrect: The modality may change the facility envelope, so existing practices need to be checked rather than assumed.

choices.text: Rank systems only by qubit count.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Qubit count does not reveal power, cooling, vibration, access, or service requirements.

choices.text: Delay facility review until installation day.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Facility review has to happen early because site, access, environmental, and service constraints can drive deployment feasibility.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Facility readiness starts by translating the modality into operational requirements.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for the answer that turns modality differences into concrete facility and operations requirements.

questions.prompt: Which factors belong in modality readiness? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Power, cooling, floor loading, and heat rejection.
Vibration, EMI, humidity, thermal, optical, or acoustic limits.
Service boundaries, access, maintenance, and recovery.
Only user-facing quantum algorithms.
Only whether the QPU fits in a standard rack.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Facility readiness combines physical envelope, environmental controls, service ownership, and operational recovery.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for facility-facing requirements, not just application demand or rack form factor.

slides.next_module_title: From Pilot Deployment to OCP Practice
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 3: Facility Readiness by Modality.

modules.title: From Pilot Deployment to OCP Practice
modules.subtitle: Operational maturity, shared infrastructure, existing OCP domains, and learner next steps.

slides.title: Making QPUs Production-Ready
slides.subtitle: How pilots become repeatable operating patterns.

slides.title: Production Criteria You Will Apply
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module you will be able to...
objectives: Explain why pilot deployments must become repeatable operating models.
Connect quantum readiness to existing OCP project areas.
Describe shared infrastructure ideas and demarcation tradeoffs.
Use a practical checklist to structure future QPU deployment conversations.

slides.title: Pilot Deployments Are Not Enough
slides.subtitle: Facilities designed today may need to host quantum systems years from now.

bullets.text: Facility design cycles, infrastructure investment horizons, and quantum hardware timelines are misaligned.
The whitepaper describes planning cycles moving from 2-3 years toward 5-10 years.
Common envelopes help operators preserve future hosting options.

figure.caption: Quantum readiness is easier to preserve during planning than to retrofit after a system arrives.
figure.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 920 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:330px;">
  <rect width="920" height="260" fill="#ffffff"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="136" x2="810" y2="136" stroke="#5F6062" stroke-width="4"/>
  <circle cx="210" cy="136" r="16" fill="#8DC63F"/><circle cx="520" cy="136" r="16" fill="#343895"/><circle cx="760" cy="136" r="16" fill="#8DC63F"/>
  <g font-family="Open Sans, Arial" fill="#333333" text-anchor="middle">
    <text x="210" y="84" font-size="22" font-weight="800">2-3 years</text><text x="210" y="108" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">traditional planning</text>
    <text x="520" y="84" font-size="22" font-weight="800">5-10 years</text><text x="520" y="108" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">emerging horizon</text>
    <text x="760" y="84" font-size="22" font-weight="800">Quantum ready</text><text x="760" y="108" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062">preserve optionality</text>
  </g>
  <text x="460" y="210" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="16" fill="#5F6062">Facility decisions made now shape which specialized compute can be hosted later.</text>
</svg>

slides.title: Operations Must Move Beyond Lab Norms
slides.subtitle: Production readiness depends on written expectations and repeatable workflows.

bullets.text: Uptime, maintenance, calibration, consumables, and access procedures need definition.
Calibration windows, quarterly maintenance, weekly service, and multi-year pump service appear across modalities.
Change control, safety, staffing, spares, and escalation paths must be planned before arrival.

figure.caption: Calibration, maintenance, consumables, and uptime expectations turn lab routines into production workflows.
figure.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 920 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:340px;">
  <defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L7,3 z" fill="#8DC63F"/></marker></defs>

</svg>

slides.title: Shared Infrastructure Can Improve Scalability

figure.caption: Figure 16 from the paper: centralized 4 K plant infrastructure can supply multiple modular millikelvin nodes, illustrating shared-resource design for scale.

slides.caption: Shared resources can reduce duplication, but they also require clear ownership, monitoring, redundancy, and maintenance procedures.

slides.title: Existing OCP Projects Are Part of Quantum Readiness
slides.subtitle: Quantum-specific requirements connect to mature infrastructure domains.

cards.title: Facility
cards.body: Vibration, EMI, zoning, access, and environmental stability.

cards.title: Hardware Management
cards.body: Discovery, configuration, telemetry, control, events, and fault handling.

cards.title: Networking
cards.body: Latency, synchronization, bandwidth, and control-plane/data-plane scale.

cards.title: Cooling Environments
cards.body: Cryogenic integration, heat rejection, and thermal stability.

cards.title: Rack and Power
cards.body: Grounding, power distribution, electrical stability, and density.

cards.title: Sustainability
cards.body: Lifecycle efficiency and resource optimization beyond pilots.

cards.title: Storage
cards.body: Workflow persistence, post-processing, and operational telemetry.

cards.title: Time Appliance
cards.body: Timing references for coordinated control and feedback.

slides.title: A Practical Readiness Checklist for Learners
slides.subtitle: Use the same categories of questions even when modalities differ.

cards.title: 1. Modality
cards.body: Which physical implementation and operating envelope?

cards.title: 2. Deployment model
cards.body: Standalone, co-located, or on-node integration?

cards.title: 3. Site envelope
cards.body: Geography, delivery, structure, utilities, and proximity.

cards.title: 4. Boundary
cards.body: Who owns facility handoffs, service access, alarms, and incidents?

cards.title: 5. Power and cooling
cards.body: Feeds, redundancy, water, heat rejection, and recovery behavior.

cards.title: 6. Environment
cards.body: Vibration, EMI, humidity, acoustic, thermal, and optical controls.

cards.title: 7. Control plane
cards.body: Discovery, allocation, scheduling, telemetry, and monitoring.

cards.title: 8. Storage
cards.body: Syndrome data, calibration traces, provenance, and retention.

cards.title: 9. Operations
cards.body: Maintenance, consumables, safety, change windows, and escalation.

slides.title: Module 4 Summary
items: Pilot deployments must become repeatable operating models.
Production quantum needs explicit boundaries, service expectations, and reliability practices.
Existing OCP domains provide much of the backbone for quantum readiness.
The right checklist makes modality differences visible and operationally actionable.

slides.title: Check Your Production Readiness Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What best shows movement from pilot toward production practice?

choices.text: Repeatable procedures, clear boundaries, monitoring, maintenance, and infrastructure integration.
A one-time successful demo.
choices.feedback_incorrect: A demo can prove feasibility, but production practice requires repeatable operations and clear handoffs.

choices.text: A lab setup with undefined facility handoffs.
choices.feedback_incorrect: Undefined handoffs are a risk. Production readiness clarifies who owns service, maintenance, access, and recovery.

choices.text: A checklist that ignores storage, networking, power, and cooling.
choices.feedback_incorrect: That misses the data center integration point. Production planning spans compute, storage, networking, power, cooling, and facilities.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Production practice is about repeatable operations across the full infrastructure stack, not only a successful experiment.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the production-readiness lens. The strongest answer includes procedures, ownership, monitoring, maintenance, and infrastructure integration.

questions.prompt: Which practices help move quantum beyond pilots? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Common checklists and reference language.
Integration with facility, power, cooling, networking, storage, and management domains.
Documented operating, maintenance, and recovery handoffs.
Treat every deployment as a one-off lab exception.
Engage facilities only after the system arrives.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. OCP-style practice makes deployments repeatable across technical and operational domains.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for practices that create repeatability, shared ownership, and cross-domain integration.

slides.course_title: Integrating Quantum Processing Units into Data Center Infrastructure
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 4: From Pilot Deployment to OCP Practice.
slides.cert_message: You have completed the full course. Continue with the whitepaper, the OCP workstream, and the FTS Quantum Track resources to follow the community work as it matures.
